title: JavaScript Mastery Encyclopedia
subtitle: Language foundations, runtime internals, browser APIs, architecture, and interview readiness for 2026.

sections.intro: Learn where JavaScript came from, how ECMAScript evolves, and how runtimes host the language.

blocks.title: What JavaScript Actually Is
blocks.html:
JavaScript is the programming language developers write, but the language is only one layer of the system. The ECMAScript specification defines syntax, values, objects, functions, modules, and runtime semantics, while each host runtime adds its own APIs and execution environment on top.

  • The language gives you variables, functions, objects, promises, classes, and modules.
  • The host decides which globals and APIs exist around the language.
  • The same core JavaScript can run in browsers, Node.js, and edge runtimes.

blocks.title: JavaScript vs ECMAScript vs Host Runtime

items.label: JavaScript
items.html: The practical language developers use every day. When people say ?JavaScript,? they usually mean the language plus the ecosystem around it.

items.label: ECMAScript
items.html: The official language standard. ECMA-262 is the authoritative spec, and the current draft includes the most recent yearly snapshot plus finished proposals that reached Stage 4.

items.label: Host runtime
items.html: The environment that executes JavaScript and exposes platform APIs. Browsers add the DOM and web APIs, Node.js adds server and system APIs, and edge runtimes tend to expose a smaller, Web-API-first surface.

blocks.title: Browser vs Node.js vs Edge runtimes
blocks.html:
You should think in terms of capabilities, not just ?frontend? or ?backend.?

  • Browsers provide the DOM, events, rendering, storage, and other web platform APIs.
  • Node.js runs JavaScript outside the browser and supports both CommonJS and ECMAScript modules, along with server, filesystem, process, stream, and networking APIs.
  • Edge runtimes often use isolate-based execution and many browser-style APIs, but usually expose fewer process and filesystem capabilities than Node.js.

blocks.title: How JavaScript Starts Running
steps: The host loads a script or resolves a module graph before execution begins.
Source code is parsed; syntax errors stop execution before the program runs.
The host creates the global execution context and establishes top-level bindings.
Declarations are processed before line-by-line evaluation, but initialization timing still differs for var, let, const, function, and class.
Top-level code executes; in module code, dependency linking and evaluation happen before the module body becomes usable to importers.

blocks.title: Script entry point vs module entry point
blocks.code:
<script src="./legacy.js"></script>
<script type="module">
  import { boot } from "./app.js";
  boot();
</script>
blocks.caption: Scripts execute directly; modules participate in a dependency graph and use import/export semantics.

blocks.title: Chapter 1 recap
items: JavaScript is the language; ECMAScript is the specification; the host runtime provides the surrounding APIs.
Browsers, Node.js, and edge runtimes share the core language but expose different capabilities.
Execution begins only after loading, parsing, binding setup, and host-specific startup rules are satisfied.

sections.intro: Build a precise mental model for values, coercion, equality, and how identity works in JavaScript.

blocks.title: Primitives, Objects, and Identity
blocks.html:
Primitive values behave like immutable values, while objects are mutable references. Two different objects can have identical shape and contents but still compare as different because equality for objects is based on identity, not structure.

  • Primitives like strings, numbers, booleans, bigint, symbol, null, and undefined are compared by value semantics.
  • Objects, arrays, and functions are reference values: assignment copies the reference, not the structure.
  • Functions are also objects, which is why they can carry properties and methods.

blocks.title: Loose Equality vs Strict Equality vs Object.is()

items.html: Loose equality may perform type conversion before comparing values. It is compact, but it also creates surprising cases such as "0" == 0 and [] == 0.
Strict equality compares without type coercion. It is the default comparison you should reach for in application code.

items.label: Object.is()
items.html: Object.is() also avoids coercion, but unlike === it treats NaN as equal to itself and distinguishes -0 from +0.

blocks.title: Coercion, conversion, and sameness
blocks.html:
JavaScript is dynamically and weakly typed, so the language often converts values when an operator or API expects a different type. Coercion is not random; it follows specification rules such as object-to-primitive conversion and numeric or string conversion depending on the operator.

  • If one operand of + is a string, concatenation wins.
  • Loose equality can coerce objects to primitives before comparing.
  • Structural similarity is never enough for object equality; two distinct objects are distinct identities.

blocks.title: Identity and coercion examples
blocks.code:
console.log({} === {});            // false
console.log(Object.is(NaN, NaN)); // true
console.log(-0 === 0);            // true
console.log(Object.is(-0, 0));    // false
console.log([] == 0);             // true
console.log([] === 0);            // false
blocks.caption: Identity, equality, and coercion are related, but they are not the same rule.

blocks.title: Coercion trap: [] == ![]
blocks.html: This expression evaluates to true. First ![] becomes false because arrays are truthy. Then the comparison becomes [] == false. Loose equality converts false to 0 and the array to a primitive, which becomes the empty string and then 0. The final comparison is 0 == 0.

blocks.title: Drill: predict the comparisons
blocks.prompt: Without running the code, predict the result of each expression: Object.is(-0, 0), NaN === NaN, Object.is(NaN, NaN), [] == 0, and [] === 0.
blocks.answerHtml: Answers: Object.is(-0, 0) is false; NaN === NaN is false; Object.is(NaN, NaN) is true; [] == 0 is true; and [] === 0 is false. The point of the drill is to separate coercion rules from identity rules.

blocks.title: Chapter 2 recap
items: Objects compare by identity, not by structural shape.
JavaScript gives you three important equality operations: ==, ===, and Object.is().
Coercion follows rules, but those rules are surprising enough that you should use them consciously, not accidentally.

sections.intro: Understand lexical scope, declaration behavior, and why closures power real JavaScript architecture.

blocks.title: Lexical Scope and Environment Records
blocks.html:
JavaScript uses lexical scoping, which means identifier lookup depends on where a variable is declared in source code, not where a function is later called. Every execution context gets an environment record, and nested code can walk outward through those environments.

  • var binds to function or global scope.
  • let and const are block scoped.
  • Modules introduce module scope, which closures can also capture.

blocks.title: How scope lookup works
steps: The current execution context checks its own environment record for the requested identifier.
If the name is not found locally, lookup walks outward to the next lexical environment.
The process repeats until the name is found or the global/module boundary is reached.
If no binding exists anywhere in the chain, JavaScript throws a ReferenceError when the identifier is accessed.

blocks.title: Closures in real architecture
blocks.html:
A closure is a function bundled with references to its surrounding lexical environment, and JavaScript creates closures every time a function is created. This is why factories, event handlers, async callbacks, and module patterns can retain private state after outer code has finished running.

  • Closures power function factories like makeAdder.
  • They are useful for encapsulation and private state.
  • They can also retain more memory than you intended if they keep references alive.

blocks.title: Closure factory and private state
blocks.caption: The returned methods keep access to count even after makeCounter() has returned.

blocks.title: Closure loop trap
blocks.html: When you create callbacks inside a loop with var, every callback closes over the same binding. By the time the callbacks run, the loop has already advanced, so all of them observe the final value instead of the per-iteration value you expected. Use let for a fresh binding each iteration or create a factory function.

blocks.title: Drill: why do all callbacks log 3?
blocks.prompt: Explain the output of: for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) { setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0); } and then explain the simplest fix.
blocks.answerHtml: It logs 3 three times because each callback closes over the same var i binding. After the loop finishes, that shared binding holds 3, so every callback reads the same final value. Replacing var with let creates a fresh per-iteration binding, which makes the callbacks log 0, 1, and 2.

blocks.title: Chapter 3 recap
items: Lexical scope depends on where bindings are declared in source code.
Closures are created when functions are created, not when they are later invoked.
The classic loop bug is a shared-binding problem, not a timer problem.

sections.intro: Go from basic functions to call-site rules, higher-order design, and invocation mechanics that interviewers probe deeply.

blocks.title: Function Forms and Invocation Patterns
blocks.html:
JavaScript has function declarations, function expressions, arrow functions, methods, constructors, and higher-order functions, but the key mastery point is this: regular function behavior depends heavily on how a function is invoked. The same function body can produce a different this value depending on its call-site.

  • Functions are callable objects.
  • Regular functions get this from the call-site.
  • Arrow functions capture this lexically from the surrounding scope.

items.label: call()
items.html: Invokes the target function immediately with an explicit this value and arguments passed one by one.

items.label: apply()
items.html: Invokes the target function immediately with an explicit this value and arguments provided as an array or array-like object.

items.label: bind()
items.html: Creates a new function whose this value is pre-bound and whose leading arguments can also be pre-filled for later calls.

blocks.title: Regular functions vs arrow functions
blocks.html:
The value of this depends on how a regular function is called, not where it was defined. Arrow functions are different: they inherit this from the surrounding scope and do not create their own this binding, which means bind(), apply(), and call() cannot retarget them.

  • Use regular functions when method-style call-site binding matters.
  • Use arrows when lexical this is the goal.
  • Do not treat arrows as ?shorter methods? without checking the binding behavior you need.

blocks.title: Invocation and this
blocks.caption: The body is the same; the call-site is what changes the outcome for regular functions.

blocks.title: `this` binding confusion
blocks.html: const fn = obj.method; fn() usually loses the original receiver because the call-site is no longer obj.method(). In strict mode, a standalone regular call produces undefined for this. This is why event handlers, timers, and extracted methods so often need wrappers, arrows, or bind().

blocks.title: Drill: what does this log?
blocks.prompt: Predict the output of: const obj = { value: 7, get() { return this.value; } }; const read = obj.get; console.log(read()); console.log(read.bind(obj)());
blocks.answerHtml: The first call logs undefined in strict mode because read() is a standalone invocation with no receiver object. The second call logs 7 because bind(obj) creates a new function whose this is permanently set to obj.

blocks.title: Function design patterns
blocks.html:
Once call-site rules are clear, you can design functions more intentionally.

  • Prefer small, explicit functions whose inputs are easy to test.
  • Use higher-order functions to parameterize behavior instead of copy-pasting logic.
  • Use partial application or binding when you want to pre-configure a function for later use.

blocks.title: Chapter 4 recap
items: Regular functions get this from the call-site; arrow functions inherit it lexically.
call() and apply() invoke immediately; bind() returns a new pre-configured function.
Most this bugs come from method extraction or callback invocation, not from syntax alone.

sections.intro: Trace how objects inherit behavior, how property lookup really works, and when classes help or hide complexity.

blocks.title: Property Lookup and Object Identity
blocks.html:
When you read obj.value, JavaScript first checks whether value exists directly on obj. If not, it walks the object's prototype chain until it finds the property or reaches null. This means objects can share behavior through prototypes without copying methods onto every instance.

  • Own properties live directly on the object itself.
  • Inherited properties are found by walking upward through the prototype chain.
  • Writing to an object usually creates or updates an own property instead of mutating an inherited one.

blocks.title: How prototype lookup works
steps: JavaScript checks the current object for an own property matching the requested key.
If the property is not present, JavaScript follows the internal prototype link to the next object in the chain.
Lookup repeats at each prototype until the property is found or the chain ends at null.
If the property is never found, the result of the lookup is undefined.

blocks.title: `new`, prototypes, and shared methods
blocks.caption: The new operator creates an object, links its prototype, invokes the constructor with that object as this, and returns the object unless the constructor explicitly returns another object.

blocks.title: Classes are syntax over prototypes
blocks.html:
class syntax changes how JavaScript code looks, but it does not replace prototypes with a different inheritance model. Instance methods still live on the prototype, constructors still initialize instance state, and extends still builds a prototype chain under the hood.

  • Classes make intent clearer and provide super, private fields, and a cleaner method syntax.
  • They do not make JavaScript a class-based language in the same sense as Java or C#.
  • Understanding the prototype model explains what classes are really doing for you.

items.label: Composition
items.html: Build objects from smaller capabilities instead of forcing them into a rigid hierarchy. Composition is often easier to evolve because you can swap or combine behaviors without deep inheritance trees.

items.label: Inheritance
items.html: Inheritance can be useful when there is a genuine "is-a" relationship, but it also couples subclasses tightly to parent behavior. In JavaScript, overusing inheritance often produces fragile class chains that are harder to reason about than composed objects or functions.

blocks.title: Prototype mutation trap
blocks.html: Changing an object's prototype shape after many instances already exist can make the system harder to reason about and can hurt performance in modern engines. Even when it is technically possible, treating prototypes as stable contracts is usually safer than mutating them at arbitrary times.

blocks.title: Chapter 5 recap
items: Property access starts on the object itself and falls back to the prototype chain only when needed.
The new operator links instances to a prototype and then runs constructor logic.
Classes improve syntax and ergonomics, but the underlying inheritance model is still prototype-based.
Composition is often the more maintainable default in JavaScript application design.

sections.intro: Choose the right collection type, reason about iteration, and understand the tradeoffs behind common data operations.

blocks.title: Array methods and iteration style
blocks.html:
Arrays are the default sequence structure in JavaScript, but mastery means choosing the right style of iteration for the problem. Methods like map, filter, reduce, some, and every make data flow easier to read when you are transforming or testing a collection.

  • Use map when every input element should produce an output element.
  • Use filter when you are selecting a subset.
  • Use loops when control flow, early exits, or side effects are the main concern.

blocks.title: Iterables vs iterators
blocks.html: An iterable is a value that knows how to create an iterator through Symbol.iterator. An iterator is the object that produces the sequence one step at a time via next(). Arrays, strings, Maps, and Sets are iterable, which is why for...of, spread syntax, and destructuring all work on them.

blocks.title: Custom iterable example
blocks.caption: Iterables create iterators. The iterator is what actually yields values during iteration.

items.label: Object
items.html: Best for simple records with a known shape, especially when property access by dot notation makes the code clearer. Objects are not ideal when you primarily need a general-purpose key-value collection.

items.label: Map
items.html: Best when keys are dynamic, when insertion order matters, or when you want collection-oriented methods like size, get, and has. Maps can use any value as a key.

items.label: Set
items.html: Best when the question is membership rather than key-value storage. Sets store unique values and are useful for deduplication, lookup, and tracking visited items.

blocks.title: Array iteration trap: for...in
blocks.html: for...in iterates enumerable property keys, not array values. On arrays it gives you string keys and may also include inherited enumerable properties. For array values, prefer for...of or array methods like map and filter.

blocks.title: Drill: pick the right collection
blocks.prompt: Choose the best structure for each scenario: a user profile with fixed fields, a cache keyed by DOM elements, a deduplicated list of visited route ids, and a value->handler registry whose keys are not limited to strings.
blocks.answerHtml: A fixed user profile is a strong fit for an object. A cache keyed by DOM elements is a good fit for a WeakMap because the keys are objects and should not be kept alive unnecessarily. A deduplicated list of visited route ids fits a Set. A value-to-handler registry with arbitrary key types fits a Map.

blocks.title: Chapter 6 recap
items: Array methods are great for data transformation, but loops are still valid when control flow matters more than elegance.
Iterables create iterators; iterators produce values one step at a time.
Use objects for records, Maps for dynamic keyed collections, and Sets for uniqueness and membership.

sections.intro: See how JavaScript is organized in modern projects, from import/export syntax to bundlers and code-splitting.

items.label: ES modules
items.html: Use import and export, are statically analyzable, and expose live bindings. That static structure is what enables tree shaking, better tooling, and module-graph analysis.

items.label: CommonJS
items.html: Uses require() and module.exports. It is still important in the Node ecosystem, but its runtime-oriented loading model makes static analysis harder and changes some interop rules.

blocks.title: How module resolution feels in practice
blocks.html:
Module resolution is the process of turning an import specifier into an actual file or package entry. In browsers, module specifiers are URL-oriented. In Node.js, package resolution includes rules around file extensions, the type field, bare specifiers, and modern package entry points like exports and imports.

  • Relative specifiers point to nearby files.
  • Bare specifiers usually refer to packages.
  • Modern Node package resolution uses package.json metadata to determine which entry point is public and which module system applies.

blocks.title: Static imports and dynamic imports
blocks.caption: Static imports participate in the module graph up front; dynamic import() creates an async loading boundary that tools can use for code splitting.

blocks.title: Bundling, tree shaking, and code splitting
blocks.html:
Bundlers analyze a module graph and package code for delivery. Tree shaking removes unused exports when the graph is statically analyzable. Code splitting breaks the graph into separately loadable chunks so you do not ship every feature on the initial page load.

  • Tree shaking works best with ESM because exports and imports are statically declared.
  • Code splitting commonly uses route boundaries or import() boundaries.
  • Bundling strategy is part of architecture: it affects performance, cacheability, and deployment behavior.

blocks.title: Module-system mismatch trap
blocks.html: Interop mistakes between ESM and CommonJS often look like missing default exports, incorrect require() usage, or environment-specific resolution failures. When a module issue appears “mysterious,” check the package's module system, file extension rules, and public entry points before assuming the import statement itself is wrong.

blocks.title: Project structure and public boundaries
blocks.html:
Mature JavaScript projects benefit from clear module boundaries. Keep internal helpers internal, publish intentional entry points, and avoid deep imports into private files unless the package explicitly documents them as stable.

  • Use folders and package entry points to communicate what is public.
  • Prefer stable import paths over reaching into internal implementation directories.
  • Think of the module graph as part of your architecture, not just a bundler detail.

blocks.title: Chapter 7 recap
items: ES modules are the modern default because they support static analysis and live bindings.
CommonJS still matters, especially in Node.js, so interop is part of real-world JavaScript mastery.
Bundling, tree shaking, and code splitting are consequences of how the module graph is structured.

sections.intro: Master promises, async functions, microtasks, timers, and the event loop behavior that explains real-world timing bugs.

blocks.title: Promises, async functions, and sequencing
blocks.html:
Promises model the eventual result of asynchronous work. async functions build on promises and let you write sequential-looking code for async steps, but the underlying control flow is still promise-based.

  • A promise can be pending, fulfilled, or rejected.
  • await pauses only the async function, not the whole runtime.
  • Promise.all() is for concurrent waiting; plain await one-after-another is sequential.

items.label: Promise.all()
items.html: Waits for every input promise to fulfill, but rejects immediately when one promise rejects. Use it when all results are required together.

items.label: Promise.allSettled()
items.html: Waits for every promise to settle and gives you per-item status. Use it when partial failure is acceptable and you want the full outcome picture.

items.label: Promise.race()
items.html: Settles as soon as the first input settles. It is useful for timeouts and winner-takes-first behavior, but it does not cancel the other work for you.

blocks.title: Microtasks vs tasks
steps: Synchronous code runs on the current call stack first.
When the stack becomes empty, the runtime flushes the microtask queue before taking the next task.
Promise reactions and queueMicrotask callbacks run as microtasks.
Timers, user events, and other host-scheduled callbacks run as tasks, so they wait until microtasks are exhausted.

blocks.title: Event loop ordering example
blocks.caption: The synchronous logs happen first, then microtasks, and only then does the timer callback run.

blocks.title: Cancellation and race conditions
blocks.html:
JavaScript promises do not cancel themselves automatically. Cancellation is usually modeled through external signals such as AbortController or by ignoring stale results when a newer request supersedes an older one.

  • Use AbortController when the underlying API supports it, such as fetch().
  • Guard against race conditions by tracking request identity or freshness.
  • Remember that Promise.race() chooses a winner, but it does not stop the losers.

blocks.title: Async iteration trap: forEach + await
blocks.html: await inside forEach() does not make the loop wait the way many developers expect, because forEach() does not await returned promises. Use for...of for sequential async loops or build an explicit promise array for concurrent work.

blocks.title: Drill: event loop ordering
blocks.prompt: Predict the output order of: console.log("start"); setTimeout(() => console.log("timeout"), 0); Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("then")); queueMicrotask(() => console.log("queueMicrotask")); console.log("end");
blocks.answerHtml: The order is start, end, then, queueMicrotask, and finally timeout. The exact relative ordering between the two microtasks depends on the order they were queued, but both microtasks run before the timer task.

blocks.title: Chapter 8 recap
items: Promises model eventual outcomes; async/await is syntax over promise-based control flow.
Microtasks run before the next task, which is why promise callbacks beat timers in common interview drills.
Cancellation and race protection require explicit design rather than hope.

sections.intro: Connect the language to the browser platform: DOM, events, storage, fetch, workers, streams, and rendering lifecycle.

blocks.title: DOM events and delegation
blocks.html:
The browser exposes the document as a tree of nodes, and events move through that tree in a defined order. Event delegation takes advantage of bubbling: instead of attaching a listener to every child, you attach one stable listener to a shared ancestor and inspect the event to decide what actually happened.

  • Delegation reduces listener churn when lists are large or dynamic.
  • Not every event bubbles, so delegation depends on the event type.
  • The DOM is a platform boundary: JavaScript describes behavior, but the browser owns parsing, layout, painting, and native event dispatch.

items.label: event.target
items.html: The original node the event was dispatched on. In delegated handlers, this is often the child that the user actually interacted with.

items.label: event.currentTarget
items.html: The node whose listener is currently running. In a delegated handler attached to a list, this stays equal to the list element even when a button inside the list was clicked.

blocks.title: Delegated event handling
blocks.caption: A single listener on the ancestor handles clicks for current and future buttons without rebinding every child.

blocks.title: Fetch, storage, and URL state
blocks.html:
Modern browser applications coordinate network, persistence, and navigation through Web APIs. fetch() is promise-based and can be cancelled with AbortController. localStorage persists per origin across sessions, while sessionStorage is scoped to the current page session. URLSearchParams, history.pushState(), and history.replaceState() let you treat the URL as part of your application's state model.

  • Prefer the URL for shareable, bookmarkable state such as filters, sort order, and pagination.
  • Prefer storage for client-only preferences that should survive reloads.
  • Treat fetch cancellation as a correctness tool, not just a performance trick, because stale responses can overwrite newer UI state.

blocks.title: What happens during event dispatch
steps: The browser creates an Event object and determines the target node.
Capture listeners run from outer ancestors toward the target if registered for capture.
Target listeners run on the target node itself.
If the event bubbles, bubble listeners then run from the target back up through ancestors.
Default browser behavior happens unless code prevented it and the event is cancelable.

blocks.title: Observers and workers
blocks.html:
Observer APIs let the browser notify you when something meaningful changes instead of forcing you to poll on every frame. IntersectionObserver helps with lazy loading and visibility-driven work, while APIs such as MutationObserver watch DOM changes. Web Workers move JavaScript off the main thread so expensive parsing, transformation, or data crunching does not block input and rendering.

  • Observers are asynchronous coordination tools; they help you react after the browser detects a threshold or mutation.
  • Workers communicate by posting messages, so design the boundary around messages and transferable data.
  • Workers can use APIs such as fetch(), but they do not get direct access to the DOM.

blocks.title: Platform trap: duplicating the same state in DOM, storage, and memory
blocks.html: Applications become fragile when the source of truth lives in too many places at once. If a filter value is stored in a DOM attribute, a local variable, localStorage, and the URL, you now have four places that can drift. Decide which layer is authoritative and derive the rest from it.

blocks.title: Chapter 9 recap
items: The browser platform provides DOM, events, networking, storage, history, and worker APIs on top of the JavaScript language.
Event delegation works because bubbling lets an ancestor observe work that originated in descendants.
Use observers and workers when the browser or a background thread can do coordination more efficiently than a tight main-thread loop.

sections.intro: Design safer code paths with clear failure handling, validation strategy, and defensive boundaries.

blocks.title: Custom errors and error boundaries
blocks.html:
Errors are part of your program's contract. A good custom error tells you what failed, where it failed, and which upstream cause triggered it. In larger systems, an error boundary is the architectural edge that catches failures, logs them, and decides whether the system can recover, retry, degrade gracefully, or must stop the current flow.

  • Extend Error when you need a domain-specific error type such as ValidationError or NetworkTimeoutError.
  • Use the cause option when rethrowing so the original failure is still inspectable.
  • Catch errors at boundaries such as request handlers, job runners, or UI task coordinators instead of scattering tiny catch blocks everywhere.

blocks.title: Wrapping an error with context
blocks.caption: Rethrow with a clearer message, but keep the original cause so debugging and logging retain the real origin.

items.label: Recoverable
items.html: The operation failed, but the application can still continue with a retry, fallback, degraded experience, or validation message. A transient network failure or invalid user input often belongs here.

items.label: Unrecoverable
items.html: The current execution path cannot continue safely. Corrupted assumptions, impossible state, or missing critical dependencies usually require aborting the current task and surfacing a high-signal failure.

blocks.title: Validation strategy and defensive coding
blocks.html:
Validate inputs at the boundaries of your system, not after bad data has already spread. Defensive coding means making invalid states harder to create, checking assumptions that come from untrusted sources, and failing in ways that preserve signal instead of hiding the problem.

  • Parse first, then validate shape, value ranges, and business rules.
  • Prefer small invariant checks near boundaries over blanket try/catch around whole features.
  • Return actionable validation feedback for user mistakes, but throw or log high-signal errors for programmer mistakes and impossible states.

blocks.title: How thrown errors travel
steps: A throw immediately stops normal execution in the current frame.
The runtime unwinds the call stack until it finds a matching catch or reaches the top-level boundary.
A finally block runs before control fully exits the try statement.
If nothing handles the failure, the runtime reports an uncaught exception or unhandled rejection.

blocks.title: Error-handling trap: swallowing the original failure
blocks.html: A catch block that logs a generic message and returns as if everything is fine destroys the debugging trail. If you transform an error, preserve the original via cause. If you cannot recover, rethrow or let the boundary handle it.

blocks.title: Drill: should you catch or rethrow?
blocks.prompt: A form submission helper catches every error from fetch, logs "something went wrong", returns null, and the caller keeps rendering as if the save succeeded. What is wrong with this pattern, and when should the helper catch versus rethrow?
blocks.answerHtml: The helper is erasing the difference between user-facing failure and success. Catch locally only if the helper can actually recover, translate the failure into a domain result, or add useful context. Otherwise preserve the failure by rethrowing so the boundary that owns the user flow can decide whether to retry, show an error state, or abort.

blocks.title: Chapter 10 recap
items: Custom errors and the Error cause option help you keep failure information precise across layers.
Recoverable failures should become explicit control flow; unrecoverable failures should surface quickly and clearly.
Defensive coding starts at boundaries: validate inputs, protect invariants, and avoid catch blocks that hide real bugs.

sections.intro: Study the engine, the garbage collector, and the performance costs that show up in real JavaScript applications.

blocks.title: Rendering cost awareness
blocks.html:
On the web, JavaScript performance is inseparable from rendering performance. Slow code does not only mean slow arithmetic; it also means delayed input handling, forced layout work, extra style recalculation, and missed frames. A useful mental model is that the main thread has to coordinate JavaScript, style, layout, paint, and compositing work while still staying responsive to user input.

  • Batch DOM reads together and DOM writes together to avoid layout thrashing.
  • Prefer doing less work over doing the same work faster.
  • Measure the actual slow path before optimizing; many perceived hot spots are not the real bottleneck.

items.label: Debounce
items.html: Wait until activity stops for a chosen delay, then run once. Use it for search inputs, autosave, or resize work where the final settled value matters more than every intermediate change.

items.label: Throttle
items.html: Allow execution at most once per interval. Use it for scroll, pointer, or resize handlers when you need periodic updates during continuous activity.

blocks.title: Debounced input work
blocks.caption: Debouncing keeps bursty input from triggering expensive work on every keystroke.

blocks.title: From interaction to visible frame
steps: A user input or timer schedules JavaScript work on the main thread.
JavaScript may read or mutate the DOM, which can trigger style and layout work.
The browser paints changed pixels and composites layers for display.
If this whole path takes too long, the browser drops or delays frames and the interface feels janky.

blocks.title: Memory leaks and garbage collection
blocks.html:
Garbage collection reclaims objects that are no longer reachable, but it cannot free memory that your code still references. Leaks are usually about unintended reachability: detached DOM nodes kept alive by listeners or arrays, long-lived caches that never evict, intervals that continue forever, or closures that hold onto more state than intended.

  • Reachability, not visual disappearance, determines whether objects can be collected.
  • WeakMap and WeakSet help for object-keyed metadata that should not keep the key alive.
  • Frequent garbage collection cycles can also hurt performance because script execution pauses while collection runs.

blocks.title: Profiling mindset
blocks.html:
Performance work begins with observation. Use browser performance tools to inspect long tasks, paint behavior, layout work, and event timing. Use memory tools to look for steadily growing heaps, detached DOM trees, or suspicious allocation patterns. Then test on the classes of devices your users actually have, because a page that feels fine on a fast laptop can still fail badly on a lower-powered phone.

  • Profile before and after changes so you know whether the optimization actually mattered.
  • Look for user-facing symptoms first: jank, lag, slow startup, or memory growth over time.
  • Treat performance as part of architecture: data shape, rendering strategy, event strategy, and code loading all contribute.

blocks.title: Performance trap: optimizing blind
blocks.html: Micro-optimizing syntax while ignoring the real bottleneck is one of the fastest ways to waste engineering time. Replacing a loop with a different loop rarely matters if the real problem is repeated layout, too many DOM nodes, a giant bundle, or a leaking cache.

blocks.title: Chapter 11 recap
items: Performance on the web is shared work between JavaScript and the browser's rendering pipeline.
Debounce and throttle solve different event-rate problems; choose based on whether the final value or periodic updates matter.
Memory issues are usually about accidental reachability, so cleanup strategy and ownership matter as much as raw code speed.

sections.intro: Learn the security mindset JavaScript developers need when code handles user input, browser APIs, and shared state.
Move from isolated language features to designing large JavaScript systems that stay understandable over time.
Use this capstone chapter for rapid recall, interview depth, debugging drills, and synthesis across the full roadmap.

qa.question: What are the primitive data types in JavaScript?
qa.answerHtml: String, Number, Boolean, BigInt, Symbol, Undefined, and Null. They are immutable and compared by value.

qa.question: What is the difference between `==` and `===`?
qa.answerHtml: `==` checks for equality with type coercion (e.g. `1 == "1"` is true). `===` is strict equality and checks if both the value and the type are identical.

qa.question: What is a closure?
qa.answerHtml: A function bundled together with its lexical environment. A closure gives you access to an outer function's scope from an inner function, even after the outer function has returned.

qa.question: What does "this" refer to in JavaScript?
qa.answerHtml: `this` refers to the object that is executing the current function. Its value is determined exactly by *how* a function is called, not where it is defined (except for arrow functions).

qa.question: What is the difference between var, let, and const?
qa.answerHtml: `var` is function-scoped and can be re-declared. `let` is block-scoped and can be reassigned. `const` is block-scoped and its reference cannot be reassigned (though objects/arrays inside it can be mutated).

qa.question: How does the map() array method work?
qa.answerHtml: It creates a new array populated with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the calling array. It does not mutate the original array.

qa.question: How does the filter() array method work?
qa.answerHtml: It creates a new array with all elements that pass the test implemented by the provided function.

qa.question: What does `async` and `await` do?
qa.answerHtml: `async` makes a function return a Promise. `await` makes an async function pause execution and wait for a Promise to resolve, making asynchronous code look synchronous.

qa.question: Why do we use strict mode (`"use strict"`)?
qa.answerHtml: It catches common coding bloopers (like using undeclared variables), disables features that are confusing, and prepares code for future ECMAScript versions.

qa.question: What is an immediately invoked function expression (IIFE)?
qa.answerHtml: A function that runs as soon as it is defined: `(function() { ... })();`. Used to create a new, private block scope to avoid polluting the global namespace.

qa.question: What is the event loop?
qa.answerHtml: The secret behind JavaScript's asynchronous behavior. It continually checks if the call stack is empty. If it is, it takes the first callback from the microtask/macrotask queue and pushes it to the stack for execution.

qa.question: What is the difference between macrotasks and microtasks?
qa.answerHtml: Microtasks (Promises, `queueMicrotask`, MutationObserver) have higher priority. The event loop empties the entire microtask queue before picking up the next macrotask (setTimeout, setInterval, user events).

qa.question: What is Debouncing?
qa.answerHtml: A pattern ensuring a function is only fired once a certain amount of time has passed since it was last invoked. Useful for search box inputs so you don't hit the API on every keystroke.

qa.question: What is Throttling?
qa.answerHtml: A pattern ensuring a function is called at most once in a specified time period. Useful for scroll or resize events so the browser doesn't crash from continuous execution.

qa.question: Explain `.bind()`, `.call()`, and `.apply()`.
qa.answerHtml: They explicitly set the value of `this`. `call` takes comma-separated arguments. `apply` takes an array of arguments. `bind` returns a *new* function with `this` permanently bound without immediately executing it.

qa.question: Why do Arrow Functions behave differently with `this`?
qa.answerHtml: Arrow functions do not have their own `this`. They inherit `this` from the surrounding lexical context at the time they are defined. You cannot `bind`, `call`, or `apply` a new `this` to an arrow function.

qa.question: What is prototypal inheritance?
qa.answerHtml: In JS, objects inherit properties and methods from a prototype object. If a property isn't found on an object, JS looks up the prototype chain until it finds it or reaches `null`.

qa.question: What causes memory leaks in JS?
qa.answerHtml: Common causes: Accidental global variables, forgotten timers/intervals (`setInterval`), closures holding onto large outer scope variables, and detached DOM elements referenced in JS objects.

qa.question: What is a WeakMap or WeakSet?
qa.answerHtml: Similar to Map/Set, but JS objects used as keys are held "weakly". This means if there are no other references to the object key, it can be garbage collected, preventing memory leaks. They cannot be iterated.

qa.question: What is the difference between Deep Copy and Shallow Copy?
qa.answerHtml: A shallow copy copies only top-level properties; nested objects share the same reference. A deep copy creates completely new references for all nested objects (e.g., using `structuredClone(obj)`).

qa.question: Explain the concept of Currying.
qa.answerHtml: Converting a function that takes multiple arguments into a sequence of functions that each take a single argument: `f(a,b,c)` becomes `f(a)(b)(c)`. Highly useful in functional composition.

qa.question: How does Service Worker caching differ from standard HTTP caching?
qa.answerHtml: HTTP caching is controlled by server headers. Service Worker caching is controlled programmatically via JavaScript using the Cache API, allowing granular offline-first strategies, bypassing the network completely.

qa.question: What is a SharedWorker vs WebWorker?
qa.answerHtml: A standard Web Worker is accessible only to the script that spawned it. A SharedWorker can be accessed by multiple scripts, even if they are in different windows, iframes, or tabs on the exact same origin.

qa.question: What is CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)?
qa.answerHtml: A security mechanism where the browser prevents a frontend JS app to make API requests to a different domain, unless the target domain explicitly returns HTTP headers letting the browser know it's permitted.

qa.question: How do JavaScript Generators (`function*`) work?
qa.answerHtml: They are functions that can be paused and exited, and later re-entered, preserving context (variable bindings) across re-entrances. They return an Iterator object, progressing only when `.next()` is called. They use the `yield` keyword.

qa.question: What does `Object.defineProperty()` do?
qa.answerHtml: It defines a new property directly on an object, or modifies an existing one, allowing strict configuration of metadata like `enumerable`, `configurable`, `writable`, or setting custom `get` and `set` traps.

qa.question: How does Proxy essentially work?
qa.answerHtml: It allows you to create a wrapper for another object, intercepting and redefining fundamental operations for it like property lookup, assignment, enumeration, and function invocation. `const p = new Proxy(target, handler)`.

qa.question: What is the purpose of `Symbol`?
qa.answerHtml: A built-in object whose constructor returns a guaranteed unique identifier. Useful to add hidden properties to an object that won't conflict with other keys or show up in standard `for...in` loops.

qa.question: What is the spread operator vs rest parameter?
qa.answerHtml: Syntax is identical (`...`). Spread EXPANDS an array/object into individual elements (e.g., `let newArr = [...old]`). Rest COLLECTS multiple elements and condenses them into a single array element (e.g., in function args: `function f(...args)`).

qa.question: What is the "void" vs "never" type?
qa.answerHtml: `void` is used for a function that returns nothing (implicitly returns undefined). `never` is used for a function that WILL NEVER return anything (e.g., it always throws an Error, or has an infinite `while(true)` loop).

qa.question: What is keyof?
qa.answerHtml: An operator that returns a Union type composed of the names of the keys of an Object type. E.g., `type Keys = keyof { name: string; age: number }` results in `"name" | "age"`.

qa.question: What does `strict: true` do in tsconfig.json?
qa.answerHtml: It enables a broad suite of strict type-checking behavior (including `noImplicitAny`, `strictNullChecks`, `strictFunctionTypes`, and `strictBindCallApply`). Highly recommended for all enterprise projects.

qa.question: What is `Promise.withResolvers()`?
qa.answerHtml: A new ES2024 feature that returns an object containing a new Promise and its `resolve` and `reject` functions. It eliminates the need to manually extract those functions from the Promise executor callback.

qa.question: How do `Object.groupBy()` and `Map.groupBy()` work?
qa.answerHtml: They allow grouping elements of an iterable based on a string or object key provided by a callback function. `Map.groupBy` is preferred when the keys should be complex objects rather than just strings.

qa.question: What is the "Task" vs "Microtask" execution order in the Event Loop?
qa.answerHtml: The loop executes ONE macrotask, then empties the ENTIRE microtask queue. If a microtask adds more microtasks, they are also executed before the next macrotask. This can lead to "microtask starvation" if not careful.

qa.question: What is the difference between `WeakRef` and `FinalizationRegistry`?
qa.answerHtml: `WeakRef` lets you hold a reference to an object without preventing it from being garbage collected. `FinalizationRegistry` lets you request a callback when an object has been garbage collected, useful for cleaning up non-JS resources.

qa.question: How does V8's "TurboFan" compiler optimize code?
qa.answerHtml: It uses Speculative Optimization. It profiles the types passing through your functions (using "Inline Caches"). If types are consistent (monomorphic), it compiles highly optimized machine code. If types change (polymorphic/megamorphic), it de-optimizes back to the "Ignition" interpreter.

qa.question: What is "Temporal Dead Zone" (TDZ) in depth?
qa.answerHtml: The period between a block's entry and the point where a variable is initialized. Accessing `let` or `const` in this zone throws a `ReferenceError`, unlike `var` which would just return `undefined`. This ensures variables are never used before their literal definition.

qa.question: What are Atomics in JavaScript?
qa.answerHtml: A global object providing atomic operations (like `add`, `sub`, `and`, `or`) for `SharedArrayBuffer`. It ensures that multi-threaded data operations in Web Workers are thread-safe and predictable without race conditions.

qa.question: How do decorators work in native JavaScript (TC39)?
qa.answerHtml: Native decorators (Stage 3+) allow class, method, and field annotations. They are functions that receive metadata about the target and can wrap or replace it. This differs from legacy "experimental" TypeScript decorators which were based on an older proposal.

qa.question: Explain "Shadow Realms" (Proposed).
qa.answerHtml: A new global environment that provides a completely isolated JS execution context. It allows running code with its own global object and built-ins, preventing side effects or data leakage into the main execution realm.

qa.question: What is `Array.fromAsync()`?
qa.answerHtml: A recent addition that creates a new array from an async iterable, iterable, or array-like object. It awaits each element if necessary, making it superior to `Promise.all(arr.map(async...))` in some sequential scenarios.

qa.question: How do you detect memory leaks using Chrome DevTools?
qa.answerHtml: Capture a "Heap Snapshot". Look for objects that are "Detached" (not in DOM but still referenced in JS). Use the "Allocation Instrumentation" timeline to see objects allocated but never released during specific interactions.

qa.question: Explain the "Pipeline Operator" (`|>`) proposal.
qa.answerHtml: It facilitates functional pipe-lining by piping the result of one expression as an argument to the next function: `val |> f |> g` is equivalent to `g(f(val))`. It significantly improves readability in nested logic.

qa.question: What is "Explicit Resource Management" (`using`) in JS?
qa.answerHtml: Introduced in ES2024, it allows for scoping resources (like file handles or DB connections) that must be closed or cleaned up. Using the `using` keyword ensures the `[Symbol.dispose]` method is called automatically when leaving scope.

qa.question: How does the "Module Preload" link relation help performance?
qa.answerHtml: It tells the browser to fetch and parse a JS module (and its entire dependency tree) early, without executing it immediately, reducing the waterfall effect in large ES Module architectures.

qa.question: What is "Hydration" in a JavaScript context?
qa.answerHtml: The process where client-side JavaScript attaches event listeners to static HTML sent from the server (SSR), making it interactive. Optimization techniques like "Resumability" (Qwik) or "Progressive Hydration" aim to reduce this costly step.

qa.question: What is the purpose of `requestIdleCallback`?
qa.answerHtml: It allows scheduling background and low-priority work during the browser's idle periods, without affecting latency-critical events like animation and input response. Ideal for non-essential telemetry or pre-fetching.

qa.question: How does `Atomics.wait()` and `Atomics.notify()` work?
qa.answerHtml: They provide a way for workers to sleep until a value in a `SharedArrayBuffer` changes. `wait` blocks the thread until notified, while `notify` wakes up threads waiting on that specific index. Essential for low-level synchronization.

qa.question: Explain the "Same-Origin Policy" (SOP) vs CORS.
qa.answerHtml: SOP is a fundamental browser security policy that prevents scripts on one origin from accessing data on another origin. CORS is a mechanism that allows servers to explicitly opt-in to sharing resources across origins.

qa.question: What is a "User-Agent Client Hints" (UA-CH)?
qa.answerHtml: A modern, more privacy-preserving alternative to the `User-Agent` string. It allows browsers to selectively share device/browser metadata with servers via HTTP headers or JS API, reducing passive fingerprinting.

qa.question: What is "BigInt" and why can't it be mixed with "Number"?
qa.answerHtml: `BigInt` handles integers larger than $2^{53}-1$. Mixing them with standard `Number` is prohibited because it would lead to silent precision loss during implicit type conversion.

qa.question: Explain "Top-level await" and its impact on module loading.
qa.answerHtml: It allows using `await` at the top level of a module. While convenient, it makes the module (and its importers) wait for the promise to resolve before execution continues, which can delay application startup if used poorly.

qa.question: What is "Metadata Reflection" (reflect-metadata)?
qa.answerHtml: A polyfill/proposal that allows attaching and retrieving metadata at runtime on classes and members. Heavily used in frameworks like NestJS or Angular for dependency injection.

qa.question: What is JavaScript?
qa.answerHtml: A high-level, interpreted, dynamically-typed programming language primarily used for web development. It runs in browsers and on servers (Node.js). It's single-threaded but non-blocking via the event loop.

qa.question: What are the 8 data types in JavaScript?
qa.answerHtml: Primitives (7): string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, null. Non-primitive (1): object (includes arrays, functions, dates, RegExp, etc.).

qa.question: What is the difference between == and ===?
qa.answerHtml: == compares after type coercion ('5'==5 is true). === compares value AND type ('5'===5 is false). Always use === to avoid unexpected bugs.

qa.question: What is the difference between null and undefined?
qa.answerHtml: undefined: declared but not assigned, missing parameter, missing property. null: intentionally empty, explicitly assigned. typeof null is 'object' (famous bug). null == undefined is true, null === undefined is false.

qa.question: What are var, let, and const?
qa.answerHtml: var: function-scoped, hoisted as undefined, can redeclare. let: block-scoped, TDZ, can reassign. const: block-scoped, must initialize, can't reassign (but object contents ARE mutable). Rule: default to const, use let for reassignment, never use var.

qa.question: What is hoisting?
qa.answerHtml: JS moves declarations to the top of their scope before execution. var is hoisted as undefined. let/const are hoisted but NOT initialized (TDZ — ReferenceError if accessed early). Function declarations are fully hoisted. Function expressions are NOT.

qa.question: What are falsy values?
qa.answerHtml: Eight values: false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, NaN. Everything else is truthy — including "0", "false", [], {}.

qa.question: What is the difference between function declaration and expression?
qa.answerHtml: Declaration: function foo(){} — fully hoisted, callable before definition. Expression: const foo = function(){} — NOT hoisted, only callable after assignment. Arrow functions are always expressions.

qa.question: What are arrow functions?
qa.answerHtml: Shorter syntax ()=>{}. Key differences: no own this (lexical), no arguments object, can't be used as constructors, no prototype. Use for callbacks; use regular functions for methods and constructors.

qa.question: What is the spread operator?
qa.answerHtml: ... expands iterables. Arrays: [...a,...b] merge, [...arr] copy. Objects: {...o1,...o2} merge. Function args: fn(...args). Rest (collecting): function(...args), const {a,...rest}=obj.

qa.question: What is destructuring?
qa.answerHtml: Extract values from arrays/objects into variables. Object: const {name, age=25} = user. Array: const [first,,third] = arr. Nested: const {address:{city}} = user. Rename: const {name:userName} = user.

qa.question: What is template literal?
qa.answerHtml: Backtick strings supporting interpolation `Hello ${name}`, multi-line strings, and tagged templates tag`text ${expr}` where tag is a function.

qa.question: What is the typeof operator?
qa.answerHtml: Returns type as string: typeof 'hi'→'string', typeof 42→'number', typeof true→'boolean', typeof undefined→'undefined', typeof null→'object' (bug!), typeof {}→'object', typeof []→'object', typeof function(){}→'function'.

qa.question: What is NaN?
qa.answerHtml: 'Not a Number' — result of invalid math (0/0, parseInt('abc')). typeof NaN is 'number'. NaN !== NaN is true (the only value not equal to itself). Check with Number.isNaN(x) (not global isNaN which coerces).

qa.question: What is the difference between for...in and for...of?
qa.answerHtml: for...in: iterates object keys (includes prototype chain — guard with hasOwnProperty). for...of: iterates values of iterables (arrays, strings, Maps, Sets — NOT plain objects). Use Object.entries() + for...of for objects.

qa.question: What are truthy and falsy values used for?
qa.answerHtml: Implicit boolean conversion in conditions: if(value), value && doSomething(), value || default, ternary value ? a : b. Common gotcha: 0 and '' are falsy but might be valid values — use ?? for null/undefined only.

qa.question: What is scope in JavaScript?
qa.answerHtml: Where variables are accessible. Global: everywhere. Function: inside the function (var). Block: inside {} (let/const). Scope chain: inner functions can access outer scope variables (not vice versa). This creates closures.

qa.question: What is a callback function?
qa.answerHtml: A function passed as an argument to another function, to be called later. Examples: setTimeout(callback, 1000), arr.map(callback), btn.addEventListener('click', callback). Callbacks enable async patterns but can lead to 'callback hell' (nested callbacks).

qa.question: What is the DOM?
qa.answerHtml: Document Object Model — a tree representation of HTML that JavaScript can manipulate. Methods: getElementById, querySelector, createElement, appendChild, addEventListener. The browser builds the DOM from HTML, then renders it visually.

qa.question: What is JSON?
qa.answerHtml: JavaScript Object Notation — a text format for data exchange. JSON.stringify(obj) converts to string. JSON.parse(str) converts back. Limitations: no functions, no undefined, no NaN/Infinity, no circular references, dates become strings.

qa.question: What is the difference between slice, splice, and split?
qa.answerHtml: slice(start,end): returns new array/string portion, doesn't mutate. splice(start,deleteCount,...items): modifies original array in-place. split(separator): string method, splits into array. Modern: use toSpliced() for immutable splice.

qa.question: What are higher-order functions?
qa.answerHtml: Functions that take functions as arguments or return functions. Examples: map, filter, reduce, forEach, sort, find, some, every. They enable functional programming patterns and method chaining.

qa.question: What is map, filter, reduce?
qa.answerHtml: map(fn): transforms each element, returns new array of same length. filter(fn): keeps elements where fn returns true, returns shorter array. reduce(fn, init): accumulates all elements into single value. All return new arrays (don't mutate original).

qa.question: What is optional chaining (?.) ?
qa.answerHtml: Short-circuits to undefined if a property is null/undefined: user?.address?.city instead of user && user.address && user.address.city. Works on properties, arrays arr?.[0], and methods fn?.().

qa.question: What is nullish coalescing (??)?
qa.answerHtml: ?? returns the right side only when left is null or undefined (not for 0 or ''). Compare: 0 || 'default' → 'default' (0 is falsy). 0 ?? 'default' → 0 (0 is not null/undefined). Use ?? when 0 or empty string are valid values.

qa.question: What is a Promise?
qa.answerHtml: An object representing eventual completion/failure of an async operation. Three states: pending → fulfilled (resolved) or rejected. Created with new Promise((resolve, reject) => {}). Consumed with .then(), .catch(), .finally(). Enables cleaner async code than callbacks.

qa.question: What is async/await?
qa.answerHtml: Syntactic sugar for Promises. async function always returns a Promise. await pauses execution until Promise resolves. Use try/catch for error handling. Makes async code look synchronous and readable.

qa.question: What is the difference between let and const for objects?
qa.answerHtml: const prevents reassignment of the variable binding, NOT mutation of the value. const obj = {} → obj.name = 'hi' works (mutation). obj = {} fails (reassignment). To truly freeze: Object.freeze(obj) — but it's shallow.

qa.question: What are default parameters?
qa.answerHtml: function greet(name = 'World') {} — default used when argument is undefined (not null). Works with destructuring: function({x = 0, y = 0} = {}) {}. Evaluated left-to-right, later params can reference earlier ones.

qa.question: What is event bubbling?
qa.answerHtml: When an event fires on an element, it 'bubbles' up through its ancestors: target → parent → grandparent → document. You can stop it with e.stopPropagation(). The opposite (capturing) goes top-down. Default is bubbling phase.

qa.question: What is the difference between innerHTML, textContent, and innerText?
qa.answerHtml: innerHTML: gets/sets HTML markup (parses tags, XSS risk). textContent: gets/sets raw text of all elements (fastest, includes hidden). innerText: gets visible text only (triggers reflow, slower). Prefer textContent for setting text safely.

qa.question: What is Array.isArray()?
qa.answerHtml: The reliable way to check if a value is an array. typeof [] returns 'object' (not helpful). [] instanceof Array fails across frames/iframes. Array.isArray([]) → true, Array.isArray({}) → false.

qa.question: What is a pure function?
qa.answerHtml: A function that: 1) always returns the same output for the same inputs, and 2) has no side effects (doesn't modify external state). const add = (a,b) => a+b is pure. Math.random() and functions that modify global variables are impure.

qa.question: What is the rest parameter?
qa.answerHtml: function sum(...nums) {} collects remaining arguments into an array. Unlike arguments (array-like, not a real array), rest params ARE real arrays. Must be the last parameter. Works in destructuring: const [first, ...rest] = arr.

qa.question: What is String interpolation?
qa.answerHtml: Embedding expressions in template literals: `Hello ${name}, you are ${age} years old`. Any valid JS expression works inside ${} — function calls, ternaries, arithmetic. Much cleaner than string concatenation with +.

qa.question: Explain closures with a practical example.
qa.answerHtml: A closure is a function that retains access to its outer scope's variables even after the outer function returns. Example: function counter() { let n = 0; return { inc: () => ++n, get: () => n }; } — the returned object 'closes over' n, keeping it alive and private. Uses: data privacy, factories, memoization, event handlers with state.

qa.question: Explain the event loop in detail.
qa.answerHtml: JS is single-threaded. The event loop: 1) Execute sync code on call stack. 2) When stack is empty, process ALL microtasks (Promise.then, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver). 3) Process ONE macrotask (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, UI rendering). 4) Repeat. Key: microtasks always run before macrotasks. Nested microtasks run immediately too (can starve macrotasks).

qa.question: Explain 'this' — all rules with priority.
qa.answerHtml: Priority (highest to lowest): 1) new: this = new object. 2) bind/call/apply: this = specified object. 3) Object method: this = calling object. 4) Default: this = window (or undefined in strict mode). Arrow functions: no own this — always inherits from enclosing scope. This is why arrows are perfect for callbacks but wrong for object methods.

qa.question: What is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?
qa.answerHtml: The period between entering a block and the let/const declaration. Accessing the variable during TDZ throws ReferenceError. Exists because let/const ARE hoisted (the engine knows about them) but NOT initialized (they have no value yet). var doesn't have TDZ — it's hoisted and initialized as undefined.

qa.question: What is event delegation?
qa.answerHtml: Attach ONE listener to a parent instead of listeners on each child. Use e.target to determine which child was clicked. Benefits: works for dynamically added elements, fewer listeners, less memory. Pattern: parent.addEventListener('click', e => { if(e.target.matches('.item')) handleClick(e.target); }).

qa.question: What is Promise.all vs allSettled vs race vs any?
qa.answerHtml: all([p1,p2]): resolves when ALL resolve, rejects on FIRST rejection. allSettled: waits for ALL, returns [{status,value/reason}], never rejects. race: returns FIRST to settle (resolve or reject). any: returns FIRST to resolve, only rejects if ALL fail (AggregateError). Use all for parallel dependent tasks, allSettled for independent tasks, race for timeouts, any for fastest mirror.

qa.question: Explain prototypal inheritance.
qa.answerHtml: Every object has [[Prototype]] link to another object. Property lookup walks the chain: object → prototype → prototype's prototype → Object.prototype → null. Object.create(proto) creates object with custom prototype. ES6 classes are syntactic sugar — extends sets up the chain, super() calls parent constructor. instanceof checks the chain.

qa.question: Shallow copy vs deep copy — methods and gotchas.
qa.answerHtml: Shallow: copies first level only. {...obj}, Object.assign({},obj), [...arr]. Nested objects share references. Deep: copies ALL levels. structuredClone(obj) (modern, handles dates/regex/maps/circular refs). JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) loses functions, undefined, dates, Infinity, NaN, circular refs. Always prefer structuredClone.

qa.question: What is the difference between call, apply, and bind?
qa.answerHtml: All set this explicitly. call(thisArg, a, b): invokes immediately, individual args. apply(thisArg, [a,b]): invokes immediately, array args. bind(thisArg, a): returns NEW function with bound this (doesn't invoke). Bind is for creating callbacks with specific context. Fun fact: Math.max.apply(null, arr) was the old way to spread — now use Math.max(...arr).

qa.question: What is a closure and how does it cause memory leaks?
qa.answerHtml: A closure retains references to outer variables. Memory leak: if a closure references a large object that's no longer needed, the GC can't collect it because the closure keeps it alive. Example: event listener closures that aren't removed. Fix: remove listeners on cleanup, null out references, use WeakRef for caches.

qa.question: Explain the classic for loop + var + setTimeout problem.
qa.answerHtml: for(var i=0;i<3;i++) setTimeout(()=>console.log(i),100) prints 3,3,3. Why: var is function-scoped — all callbacks share the SAME i, which is 3 after the loop finishes. Fixes: 1) Use let (block-scoped, new binding per iteration). 2) IIFE: ((j)=>setTimeout(()=>console.log(j),100))(i). 3) setTimeout(console.log, 100, i) (third arg).

qa.question: What are Map and Set? When use over Object/Array?
qa.answerHtml: Map: any key type, ordered, iterable, .size, no prototype pollution. Use when: keys are objects/functions, you need ordered iteration, frequent add/delete. Set: unique values only, .has() is O(1). Use for: deduplication [...new Set(arr)], membership testing. Object/Array are still better for JSON data and simple cases.

qa.question: What is debounce vs throttle?
qa.answerHtml: Debounce: waits until user STOPS doing something for N ms. Use: search input, resize handler. Throttle: executes at most once every N ms. Use: scroll handler, rate-limiting API calls. Debounce delays, throttle caps. Both use closures + setTimeout internally.

qa.question: What is the difference between Object.keys, values, entries?
qa.answerHtml: Object.keys(obj): array of own enumerable string keys. Object.values(obj): array of own enumerable values. Object.entries(obj): array of [key, value] pairs. None include Symbol keys or prototype properties. For Maps: use map.keys(), map.values(), map.entries().

qa.question: What is a generator function?
qa.answerHtml: function* gen() { yield 1; yield 2; } — can pause and resume. Returns an iterator with .next() → {value, done}. Use cases: lazy evaluation, infinite sequences, implementing custom iterables, controlled async flow. yield* delegates to another generator.

qa.question: Explain async iteration (for await...of).
qa.answerHtml: for await (const item of asyncIterable) {} — iterates over async iterables (streams, paginated APIs). The iterable's [Symbol.asyncIterator]() returns objects with async next() methods. Use with async generators: async function* fetchPages() { while(hasMore) { yield await fetchPage(); } }

qa.question: What is memoization?
qa.answerHtml: Caching function results based on inputs to avoid redundant computation. Implementation: function memoize(fn) { const cache = new Map(); return (...args) => { const key = JSON.stringify(args); if(cache.has(key)) return cache.get(key); const result = fn(...args); cache.set(key, result); return result; }; } Uses closures to persist the cache.

qa.question: What are tagged template literals?
qa.answerHtml: tag`text ${expr}` where tag is a function receiving strings array and interpolated values. Used by: styled-components (css`color: red`), GraphQL (gql`query{}`), i18n, SQL sanitization. The tag function can transform or validate the template. You can return anything, not just strings.

qa.question: What is the difference between Error types?
qa.answerHtml: Error: base class. TypeError: wrong type (null.prop). ReferenceError: undeclared variable. SyntaxError: parse error. RangeError: value out of range. URIError: bad URI. Custom: class AppError extends Error { constructor(msg,code) { super(msg); this.code=code; } }

qa.question: What is Symbol and when do you use it?
qa.answerHtml: Unique, immutable identifier: const id = Symbol('description'). Every Symbol() is unique. Not visible in for...in, Object.keys(), JSON.stringify(). Use cases: private-ish object keys, avoiding name collisions in libraries, well-known symbols (Symbol.iterator, Symbol.toPrimitive).

qa.question: Explain Object.freeze, seal, preventExtensions.
qa.answerHtml: Object.freeze(): no add, no delete, no modify — completely locked (shallow). Object.seal(): no add, no delete, CAN modify existing. Object.preventExtensions(): no add, CAN delete and modify. All are shallow — nested objects remain mutable. Object.isFrozen/isSealed/isExtensible to check.

qa.question: What is the arguments object?
qa.answerHtml: Array-like object available in regular functions containing all passed arguments. NOT available in arrow functions. NOT a real array (no map/filter). Convert: [...arguments] or Array.from(arguments). Modern replacement: rest parameters (...args) — always use rest instead.

qa.question: What is strict mode?
qa.answerHtml: Enabled with 'use strict'. Effects: prevents accidental globals (undeclared = error), disables with statement, makes eval safer, prevents duplicate params, this is undefined (not window) in regular functions, prohibits deleting non-configurable properties. Modules and classes are always strict.

qa.question: What is currying?
qa.answerHtml: Transforming f(a,b,c) into f(a)(b)(c). Each call returns a new function until all arguments are collected. Use: partial application const add5 = curry(add)(5), creating reusable utilities, point-free programming. Libraries like lodash/ramda provide curry helpers.

qa.question: What is the Proxy object?
qa.answerHtml: Intercepts operations on objects: get, set, delete, has, apply, construct, etc. new Proxy(target, handler). Real uses: validation (reject invalid values), reactive systems (Vue.js), logging/debugging, access control, default values for missing properties, negative array indexing.

qa.question: What is Reflect?
qa.answerHtml: Built-in object with methods matching Proxy trap names: Reflect.get(), Reflect.set(), Reflect.has(), etc. Provides the default behavior that Proxy traps can call. More reliable than direct operations — Reflect.defineProperty() returns boolean instead of throwing like Object.defineProperty().

qa.question: How does garbage collection work in JavaScript?
qa.answerHtml: JS uses mark-and-sweep: the GC starts from roots (global, stack), marks all reachable objects, then sweeps (frees) unreachable ones. Common leak patterns: forgotten timers/intervals, detached DOM references in closures, growing arrays/maps never cleaned, circular references (handled by modern GC but still add pressure).

qa.question: What are WeakMap and WeakSet?
qa.answerHtml: WeakMap: keys must be objects, weakly held — GC can collect keys if no other references exist. Not iterable, no .size. Use: private data per object, caching without leaks. WeakSet: objects only, weakly held. Use: tracking visited objects, tagging objects without preventing GC.

qa.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous iteration?
qa.answerHtml: Sync: for...of with [Symbol.iterator], yields values immediately. Async: for await...of with [Symbol.asyncIterator], yields Promises. Async iterators are created with async function* generators. Use for: reading streams, paginated APIs, processing data as it arrives.

qa.question: What is the new keyword doing under the hood?
qa.answerHtml: 1) Creates a new empty object. 2) Sets its [[Prototype]] to the constructor's .prototype. 3) Calls the constructor with this = the new object. 4) Returns the object (unless constructor explicitly returns an object). That's why arrow functions can't be constructors — they have no .prototype and can't bind this.

qa.question: What is structuredClone?
qa.answerHtml: Modern deep cloning: structuredClone(obj). Handles: nested objects, arrays, Maps, Sets, Dates, RegExp, ArrayBuffers, circular references. Does NOT handle: functions, DOM nodes, Error objects, property descriptors, prototype chain. Available in all modern browsers and Node.js 17+.

qa.question: Explain Promise chaining vs async/await.
qa.answerHtml: fetchUser().then(u => fetchPosts(u.id)).then(posts => render(posts)).catch(handleError) vs try { const u = await fetchUser(); const posts = await fetchPosts(u.id); render(posts); } catch(e) { handleError(e); } — async/await is cleaner for sequential operations. Promise.all + await is best for parallel operations.

qa.question: What is Object.defineProperty?
qa.answerHtml: Defines or modifies a property with fine-grained control: Object.defineProperty(obj, 'name', { value: 'Alice', writable: false, enumerable: true, configurable: false }). Also supports getter/setter: { get() { return this._name; }, set(v) { this._name = v; } }. Foundation for Vue 2's reactivity system.

qa.question: What are Iterables and Iterators?
qa.answerHtml: Iterable: object with [Symbol.iterator]() method (strings, arrays, Maps, Sets). Iterator: object with .next() → {value, done}. Custom: const iterable = { [Symbol.iterator]() { let i=0; return { next() { return i<3 ? {value:i++,done:false} : {done:true}; } }; } }. Enables for...of, spread, destructuring.

qa.question: What is the difference between setTimeout(fn,0) and queueMicrotask(fn)?
qa.answerHtml: setTimeout(fn,0): adds to macrotask queue — runs after ALL microtasks and any pending rendering. queueMicrotask(fn): adds to microtask queue — runs before any macrotasks, after current synchronous code. Promise.then callbacks are also microtasks. This order difference matters for timing-sensitive code.

qa.question: What is a Web Worker?
qa.answerHtml: Runs JS in a background thread — true parallelism. Created with new Worker('worker.js'). Communicate via postMessage/onmessage. Workers can't access DOM, window, or document. Use for: heavy computation, data processing, image manipulation. SharedWorker is shared across tabs. Service Worker handles network requests (offline, caching).

qa.question: What are ES2023-2024 new features?
qa.answerHtml: ES2023: Array.findLast/findLastIndex, toSorted/toReversed/toSpliced/with (immutable array methods), hashbang grammar. ES2024: Object.groupBy(), Map.groupBy(), Promise.withResolvers(), well-formed Unicode strings, Atomics.waitAsync. These are actively asked in senior interviews.

qa.question: What is the module system (import/export)?
qa.answerHtml: ES Modules: export const x = 1, export default fn, import {x} from './mod', import fn from './mod'. Live bindings (exports reflect current value). Static analysis (tree-shakable). import() for dynamic/lazy loading. CommonJS (require/module.exports) is Node.js legacy — synchronous, not tree-shakable.

qa.question: What is the difference between deep equality and shallow equality?
qa.answerHtml: Shallow: compares first-level values with ===. {a:1} !== {a:1} (different references). Deep: recursively compares all nested values. No built-in deep equality in JS. Options: JSON.stringify(a) === JSON.stringify(b) (order-dependent, loses types), lodash _.isEqual(), custom recursive function. React uses Object.is() for state comparison.

qa.question: What is short-circuit evaluation?
qa.answerHtml: &&: returns first falsy value (or last if all truthy). ||: returns first truthy value (or last if all falsy). Uses: user && user.name (safe access), name || 'Anonymous' (default). Gotcha: 0 || 'default' gives 'default' because 0 is falsy — use ?? for null/undefined only.

qa.question: Implement debounce from scratch.
qa.answerHtml: function debounce(fn, delay) { let timer; return (...args) => { clearTimeout(timer); timer = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), delay); }; } Uses closure to persist timer. Each call resets the timer. The function only fires after the user stops calling for the specified delay. Can be enhanced with leading/trailing options and cancel method.

qa.question: Implement throttle from scratch.
qa.answerHtml: function throttle(fn, limit) { let inThrottle; return (...args) => { if (!inThrottle) { fn(...args); inThrottle = true; setTimeout(() => inThrottle = false, limit); } }; } Ensures function runs at most once per interval. First call executes immediately. Uses closure for the flag. Can be enhanced with trailing call option.

qa.question: Implement a simple Promise from scratch.
qa.answerHtml: Core: store state (pending/fulfilled/rejected), value, and callback queue. resolve() transitions state and runs stored callbacks. .then() returns a new Promise (for chaining), stores callbacks if pending, runs immediately if already resolved. Key: callbacks must be async (microtask) per spec. This demonstrates understanding of the Promise specification (Promises/A+).

qa.question: Implement curry function.
qa.answerHtml: const curry = (fn) => { const arity = fn.length; return function curried(...args) { return args.length >= arity ? fn(...args) : (...more) => curried(...args, ...more); }; } Recursively collects arguments until enough are gathered. curry(add)(1)(2) and curry(add)(1,2) both work. Placeholder support adds complexity.

qa.question: Implement Function.prototype.bind polyfill.
qa.answerHtml: Function.prototype.myBind = function(context, ...boundArgs) { const fn = this; return function(...args) { return fn.apply(context, [...boundArgs, ...args]); }; } Uses closure to capture context and partial args. Real bind also handles 'new' calls and preserves prototype chain — this simplified version covers 90% of interview expectations.

qa.question: Explain the JavaScript engine pipeline.
qa.answerHtml: 1) Parsing: source → AST (Abstract Syntax Tree). 2) Interpretation: AST → bytecode (executed immediately by interpreter). 3) Profiling: engine monitors 'hot' functions (called frequently). 4) JIT Compilation: hot functions compiled to optimized machine code. 5) Deoptimization: if assumptions are wrong (e.g., type changed), falls back to interpreter. V8 uses Ignition (interpreter) + TurboFan (compiler).

qa.question: What are memory leaks in JavaScript? How to detect and fix?
