qa.answerHtml: Common leaks: 1) Forgotten timers/intervals (clear them!). 2) Detached DOM references in closures. 3) Closures retaining large objects unnecessarily. 4) Growing caches without eviction. 5) Event listeners not removed. Detect: Chrome DevTools Memory tab — take heap snapshots, compare, find growing objects. Fix: cleanup functions, WeakMap/WeakRef for caches, remove listeners, null out references.

qa.question: Explain the Proxy/Reflect API with real use cases.
qa.answerHtml: Proxy intercepts 13 operations: get, set, has, deleteProperty, apply, construct, etc. Reflect provides default behavior for each trap. Use cases: 1) Validation: reject invalid values in set trap. 2) Reactive systems: trigger UI updates on property change (Vue 3's reactivity). 3) Negative indexing: arr[-1]. 4) Auto-vivification: auto-create nested objects on access. 5) Access logging: track all property reads/writes.

qa.question: Explain JavaScript module systems (ESM vs CJS vs AMD vs UMD).
qa.answerHtml: ESM: import/export, static analysis, tree-shakable, async loading, live bindings. CJS: require/module.exports, Node.js legacy, synchronous, copies values. AMD: define([],(deps)=>{}), browser async loading (RequireJS), mostly obsolete. UMD: wrapper that works in all systems (CJS/AMD/global). Modern: use ESM everywhere. Node.js supports both via .mjs extension or package.json "type":"module".

qa.question: What is tail call optimization (TCO)?
qa.answerHtml: When the last action of a function is a function call, the engine can reuse the current stack frame instead of adding a new one — prevents stack overflow for recursive functions. Only Safari implements TCO in strict mode. Other engines don't. Workaround: convert recursion to iteration, or use trampolining: function trampoline(fn) { let result = fn(); while(typeof result === 'function') result = result(); return result; }

qa.question: Implement EventEmitter from scratch.
qa.answerHtml: class EventEmitter { constructor() { this.events = {}; } on(event, fn) { (this.events[event] ??= []).push(fn); return this; } off(event, fn) { this.events[event] = this.events[event]?.filter(f => f !== fn); } emit(event, ...args) { this.events[event]?.forEach(fn => fn(...args)); } once(event, fn) { const wrapper = (...args) => { fn(...args); this.off(event, wrapper); }; this.on(event, wrapper); } } Demonstrates closures, this binding, and the observer pattern.

qa.question: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism in JS?
qa.answerHtml: Concurrency: multiple tasks make progress (event loop — one thread, interleaved execution). Parallelism: multiple tasks run simultaneously (Web Workers — multiple threads). JS main thread is concurrent, not parallel. Web Workers enable true parallelism. SharedArrayBuffer + Atomics enable shared memory between workers (with synchronization).

qa.question: Explain WeakRef and FinalizationRegistry.
qa.answerHtml: WeakRef: holds weak reference to object. ref.deref() returns object or undefined if GC'd. Use for caches that shouldn't prevent garbage collection. FinalizationRegistry: register callbacks that fire when objects are GC'd. const registry = new FinalizationRegistry(heldValue => { cleanup(heldValue); }); registry.register(obj, 'cleanup-token'). Both are non-deterministic — GC timing is unpredictable.

qa.question: Implement deep clone without structuredClone.
qa.answerHtml: function deepClone(obj, seen = new WeakMap()) { if (obj === null || typeof obj !== 'object') return obj; if (seen.has(obj)) return seen.get(obj); if (obj instanceof Date) return new Date(obj); if (obj instanceof RegExp) return new RegExp(obj); const clone = Array.isArray(obj) ? [] : {}; seen.set(obj, clone); for (const key of Reflect.ownKeys(obj)) { clone[key] = deepClone(obj[key], seen); } return clone; } Handles circular refs with WeakMap, preserves Date/RegExp, uses Reflect.ownKeys for symbols.

qa.question: What is the Temporal API (Stage 3)?
qa.answerHtml: Modern date/time API replacing the broken Date object. Temporal.Now.instant(), Temporal.PlainDate.from('2025-01-15'), Temporal.ZonedDateTime. Features: immutable, timezone-aware, calendar support, duration arithmetic, no month-indexing issues (January is 1 not 0). Still Stage 3 but polyfillable. Will eventually obsolete moment.js and date-fns for most use cases.

qa.question: Implement pipe and compose functions.
qa.answerHtml: const pipe = (...fns) => (x) => fns.reduce((v, fn) => fn(v), x); Left-to-right composition. const compose = (...fns) => (x) => fns.reduceRight((v, fn) => fn(v), x); Right-to-left. Usage: const transform = pipe(trim, lowercase, capitalize); transform(' HELLO '). Fundamental functional programming pattern. TC39 has a pipeline operator proposal (x |> fn).

qa.question: Explain the Iterator and Iterable protocols in depth.
qa.answerHtml: Iterable: has [Symbol.iterator]() returning an iterator. Iterator: has .next() returning {value, done}. Custom: make any object iterable. Async versions use [Symbol.asyncIterator]() and async next(). Generators auto-implement both protocols. Consumed by: for...of, spread, destructuring, Array.from(), Promise.all(), yield*.

qa.question: What is the structured clone algorithm?
qa.answerHtml: The algorithm behind structuredClone(), postMessage(), IndexedDB, and history API. Handles: objects, arrays, Maps, Sets, Dates, RegExp, ArrayBuffers, Blobs, Files, ImageData, circular references. Does NOT handle: functions, DOM nodes, property descriptors, getters/setters, prototype chain, Error objects (in some engines). It's what makes Web Worker communication possible.

qa.question: Explain AbortController and AbortSignal.
qa.answerHtml: const controller = new AbortController(); fetch(url, {signal: controller.signal}).catch(e => { if(e.name === 'AbortError') console.log('Cancelled'); }); controller.abort(); Cancels fetch requests, event listeners, and any async operation that accepts a signal. Multiple operations can share one signal. Use in React cleanup: useEffect(() => { const ctrl = new AbortController(); fetch(url, {signal: ctrl.signal}); return () => ctrl.abort(); }, [url])

qa.question: What are SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics?
qa.answerHtml: SharedArrayBuffer: memory shared between main thread and workers (unlike regular ArrayBuffer which is transferred/copied). Atomics: provides thread-safe operations on shared memory — Atomics.add(), Atomics.load(), Atomics.store(), Atomics.wait()/notify() for synchronization. Required for high-performance parallel computing in JS. Requires cross-origin isolation headers (COOP/COEP) due to Spectre mitigations.
